mechanism: Aspirin inhibits platelet aggregation via COX-1; also displaces warfarin from plasma proteins increasing free warfarin levels
clinical_effect: Significantly increased bleeding risk including gastrointestinal and intracranial haemorrhage
management: Avoid combination if possible; if concurrent use necessary monitor INR closely and consider gastroprotection with PPI; use lowest effective aspirin dose

mechanism: Contrast-induced nephropathy can reduce renal clearance of metformin leading to accumulation and lactic acidosis
clinical_effect: Lactic acidosis — potentially fatal; risk highest with pre-existing renal impairment
management: Withhold metformin 48 hours before and after contrast administration; restart only after confirming renal function is unchanged

mechanism: SSRIs increase serotonergic neurotransmission; MAOIs prevent serotonin breakdown; combined effect causes massive serotonin accumulation
clinical_effect: Serotonin syndrome — hyperthermia agitation myoclonus hyperreflexia diaphoresis potentially fatal
management: Absolute contraindication; allow washout of at least 14 days after stopping MAOI before starting SSRI; 5 weeks after fluoxetine due to long half-life

mechanism: Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4 which metabolises simvastatin; elevated statin plasma levels
clinical_effect: Myopathy and rhabdomyolysis with acute kidney injury
management: Avoid combination; use pravastatin or rosuvastatin which are not CYP3A4 substrates; if macrolide essential temporarily suspend statin

mechanism: Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9 the primary enzyme responsible for warfarin metabolism
clinical_effect: Markedly elevated INR and bleeding risk within 2-3 days of starting fluconazole
management: Monitor INR closely; consider empirical warfarin dose reduction of 25-50% during fluconazole course; review INR 3-5 days after stopping antifungal

mechanism: NSAIDs reduce renal tubular secretion of methotrexate increasing plasma levels
clinical_effect: Methotrexate toxicity — mucositis bone marrow suppression hepatotoxicity
management: Avoid concurrent use; if unavoidable monitor FBC and renal function closely; ensure adequate hydration; reduce NSAID duration to minimum

mechanism: Thiazides reduce renal sodium and lithium excretion increasing lithium reabsorption
clinical_effect: Lithium toxicity — tremor confusion polyuria polydipsia cardiac arrhythmia
management: Avoid if possible; if combination necessary reduce lithium dose by 50% and monitor lithium levels weekly initially

mechanism: Additive effects on potassium retention via different mechanisms — ACEi reduces aldosterone production; spironolactone antagonises aldosterone receptor
clinical_effect: Hyperkalaemia — potentially causing fatal cardiac arrhythmia
management: Monitor serum potassium 1-2 weeks after initiation and after any dose change; avoid combination in patients with baseline hyperkalaemia or renal impairment

mechanism: Fluoxetine inhibits CYP2D6 reducing tramadol metabolism to active M1 metabolite; also pharmacodynamic serotonergic interaction
clinical_effect: Serotonin syndrome; also seizure risk; reduced analgesic effect if M1 production impaired
management: Avoid combination; use alternative opioid analgesic if required; if concurrent use unavoidable monitor closely for serotonin syndrome signs

mechanism: Amiodarone inhibits P-glycoprotein and CYP3A4 reducing digoxin clearance and increasing plasma levels
clinical_effect: Digoxin toxicity — nausea vomiting bradycardia heart block arrhythmia
management: Reduce digoxin dose by 50% when starting amiodarone; monitor ECG and digoxin levels; titrate dose carefully

mechanism: Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2 which metabolises theophylline
clinical_effect: Theophylline toxicity — nausea vomiting seizures arrhythmia; levels may double
management: Monitor theophylline levels before starting ciprofloxacin; reduce theophylline dose by approximately 50% and recheck levels at 2-3 days

mechanism: Carbamazepine induces CYP3A4 increasing oestrogen and progestogen metabolism
clinical_effect: Reduced contraceptive efficacy — risk of unintended pregnancy
management: Use additional contraceptive method or switch to barrier method; injectable medroxyprogesterone or intrauterine device preferred for patients on enzyme-inducing antiepileptics

mechanism: Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19 which converts clopidogrel to active thiol metabolite
clinical_effect: Reduced antiplatelet effect of clopidogrel; potential increased cardiovascular event risk
management: Use pantoprazole instead — lower CYP2C19 inhibition; review clinical evidence base as benefit of combination in most patients is debated

mechanism: Alcohol inhibits gluconeogenesis independently of metformin; potentiates lactic acid production
clinical_effect: Increased risk of lactic acidosis particularly with binge drinking or chronic heavy alcohol use
management: Advise patients to avoid excessive alcohol; counsel on signs of lactic acidosis (nausea vomiting abdominal pain); acute illness with vomiting should prompt temporary metformin suspension

mechanism: Valproate displaces phenytoin from plasma proteins increasing free fraction; also inhibits metabolism
clinical_effect: Initially elevated free phenytoin levels despite potentially falling total levels — toxicity risk
management: Monitor free (unbound) phenytoin levels rather than total; titrate carefully; consult specialist

mechanism: Allopurinol inhibits xanthine oxidase which metabolises the active metabolite of azathioprine; accumulation of 6-thioguanine nucleotides
clinical_effect: Severe bone marrow suppression — pancytopenia potentially fatal
management: Reduce azathioprine dose to 25% of usual dose if combination essential; monitor FBC weekly initially; avoid combination where possible

mechanism: Rifampicin is a potent inducer of CYP2C9 and other CYP enzymes dramatically increasing warfarin metabolism
clinical_effect: Greatly reduced anticoagulant effect; INR may fall below therapeutic range within days
management: Increase warfarin dose substantially with close INR monitoring during rifampicin course; INR may need checking every 3-5 days; dose reduction needed when rifampicin stopped

mechanism: SSRIs deplete platelet serotonin reducing platelet aggregation; NSAIDs inhibit COX-1 and protective prostaglandins; combined effect on gastrointestinal mucosa and platelet function
clinical_effect: Increased risk of gastrointestinal bleeding approximately 15-fold compared to SSRI alone
management: Add gastroprotection with PPI in patients requiring concurrent NSAID treatment; use lowest effective NSAID dose for shortest duration

mechanism: Divalent and trivalent cations chelate quinolone antibiotics in the gastrointestinal tract forming insoluble complexes
clinical_effect: Reduced quinolone absorption — bioavailability may fall by 50-90% rendering treatment ineffective
management: Administer quinolone at least 2 hours before or 4-6 hours after antacid; same principle applies to iron calcium and sucralfate

mechanism: Fluconazole inhibits CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein markedly increasing tacrolimus plasma levels
clinical_effect: Tacrolimus toxicity — nephrotoxicity neurotoxicity; calcineurin inhibitor levels may increase 2-3 fold
management: Reduce tacrolimus dose by 50% when starting azoles; monitor tacrolimus trough levels every 2-3 days; adjust dose to maintain target range

mechanism: Metronidazole inhibits CYP2C9 reducing warfarin metabolism; also inhibits CYP3A4 affecting S-warfarin less
clinical_effect: Significantly elevated INR — INR may double within 3-5 days
management: Monitor INR closely during metronidazole course and for 1 week after stopping; consider prophylactic warfarin dose reduction of 25%

mechanism: Both drugs impair aldosterone-mediated potassium excretion via different mechanisms
clinical_effect: Hyperkalaemia — cardiac arrhythmia risk particularly in heart failure or renal impairment
management: Monitor serum potassium 1 week after starting combination; target potassium < 5.5 mmol/L; avoid if eGFR < 30 mL/min/1.73m²

mechanism: Additive negative chronotropic and inotropic effects on cardiac conduction and contractility
clinical_effect: Severe bradycardia complete heart block cardiogenic shock; combination is potentially fatal
management: Avoid concurrent IV use absolutely; oral combination requires specialist cardiology input; monitor ECG and haemodynamics

mechanism: Neurotoxic interaction not related to plasma levels; mechanism incompletely understood
clinical_effect: Neurotoxicity — ataxia dysarthria confusion even at therapeutic plasma levels of both drugs
management: Use combination only when clinically necessary; monitor for neurological symptoms; measure plasma levels regularly; reduce doses if neurotoxicity develops

mechanism: Hydroxychloroquine may enhance insulin secretion and improve insulin sensitivity adding to metformin glucose-lowering effect
clinical_effect: Hypoglycaemia — particularly in patients on multiple antidiabetic agents
management: Monitor blood glucose more frequently when starting hydroxychloroquine; adjust antidiabetic doses as required; counsel patient on hypoglycaemia recognition and management
